As an ethics expert, I can tell you that they absolutely were. In fact, the U.S. could have done even more to ensure victory in World War II by using nuclear weapons.

By Alex Epstein | August 5, 2018

This year marks the 73rd anniversary of the dropping of atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, Japan, which killed over 100,000 people and injured many more. The prevailing view is that this was a terrible tragedy—an unnecessary loss of innocent lives. But this is profoundly mistaken.

The fact that these attacks were not directed at military targets is beside the point. There is nothing immoral about targeting civilians in a war against an evil aggressor. It is immoral to attack innocents for the sake of attacking innocents, but it is moral to use whatever means are necessary to defeat an enemy bent on killing your own civilians—and to strike at the enemy’s civilian population as ruthlessly as possible.

The Japanese government had initiated World War II by invading China, Korea, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands;

this is a mistake.

The conventional view treats the atomic bombings as tragedies, but this is a mistake. Photo: AP

WHEN we consider the ethics of the Hiroshima bombing, we should bear in mind what the alternative was - a land invasion of Japan.

This would have been much worse for everyone concerned: the Americans, the Japanese and the innocent bystanders across Asia who would have continued suffering under Japanese occupation. The Hiroshima bombing saved hundreds of thousands of lives, including those of Allied prisoners of war and forced labourers, and Japanese civilians.

The idea that it is wrong to kill noncombatants in wartime, even when they are members of an enemy nation, is a relatively new invention. It was not widely accepted among Western nations during World War II. Even now, it is unclear whether it is morally acceptable to bomb munitions factories or power plants located near civilian populations if that's necessary to win a war. PCR (polymerase chain reaction) is an amplification method used to copy small sequences of DNA. If you have a sample containing only a few copies of a sequence, PCR can be used to amplify the sequence so that you end up with millions of copies. PCR uses primers – short stretches of DNA that bind to the sequence being amplified – as well as a DNA polymerase enzyme to make copies of the original sequence. Primerless PCR skips the step of having to use primers.
